skill.name: api-fuzzing-bug-bounty
skill.description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "test API security", "fuzz APIs", "find IDOR vulnerabilities", "test REST API", "test GraphQL", "API penetration testing", "bug bounty hunting", or similar security testing requests.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for API fuzzing and bug bounty hunting. Learn to test REST, SOAP, and GraphQL APIs for IDOR, injection vulnerabilities, and authentication bypasses.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 76 static findings are false positives. The skill is documentation for authorized security testing (bug bounty/penetration testing). The detected patterns are legitimate testing methodologies: command examples for API fuzzing, example URLs for target testing, and documented vulnerability test payloads (XXE, LFI, SSRF). This is standard security education content for authorized testing workflows.

critical_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positive: XXE Test Payload
critical_findings.description: Line 141 contains `<!DOCTYPE test [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>` which is a legitimate XXE (XML External Entity) injection test payload. This is standard security testing documentation for authorized penetration testing, not actual malicious code.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a documented XXE test payload commonly used in authorized security testing. The skill explicitly teaches bug bounty and penetration testing techniques.

critical_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positive: LFI Test Payload
critical_findings.description: Line 298 contains `<iframe src="file:///etc/passwd"` which is a Local File Inclusion (LFI) test example. This is legitimate documentation for testing PDF export vulnerabilities.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a documented LFI testing technique commonly taught in penetration testing courses and CTF challenges.

high_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positive: JWT Weakness Documentation
high_findings.description: Line 329 mentions 'JWT Weaknesses' in the vulnerability checklist. This documents what to test for during security assessments, not the use of weak cryptography.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a checklist item for penetration testers to identify weak JWT implementations, which is legitimate security testing content.

content.value_statement: APIs often contain security vulnerabilities that attackers can exploit. This skill teaches comprehensive API fuzzing techniques so you can identify IDOR, injection flaws, and authentication bypasses during authorized bug bounty hunting.
actual_capabilities: Identify and enumerate REST, SOAP, and GraphQL API endpoints
Test for IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerabilities with multiple bypass techniques
Perform injection testing including SQL injection, command injection, and XXE in JSON and XML payloads
Bypass authentication and authorization controls using method tampering and content type switching
Exploit GraphQL-specific vulnerabilities including introspection abuse, batching attacks, and nested query DoS
Discover undocumented endpoints and test API version differences for security gaps
limitations: Requires authorization from the target owner before testing any API
Does not provide actual exploit execution - only testing methodology and guidance
Skill does not include built-in scanning tools - user must use external tools like Burp Suite
Does not cover mobile API specific testing beyond general guidance

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Hunter Tests Target API
use_cases.description: A bug bounty researcher uses this skill to systematically test a program's API for vulnerabilities. They follow the IDOR testing workflow, injection techniques, and GraphQL testing methods to find valid security issues.
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters and independent security researchers

use_cases.title: Penetration Tester Assesses API Security
use_cases.description: A professional penetration tester uses this skill during an engagement to audit client APIs. The skill provides a comprehensive checklist and methodology for testing API security controls.
use_cases.target_user: Professional penetration testers and security consultants

use_cases.title: Developer Learns API Security
use_cases.description: A software developer uses this skill to understand common API vulnerabilities. This helps them write more secure code and better understand how to defend against the attack techniques documented here.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers learning about API security

prompt_templates.title: Basic API Security Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me test a REST API endpoint for security vulnerabilities. The endpoint is /api/v1/users/{id} and uses Bearer token authentication. What are the main vulnerability categories I should test for?
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to understand what to test on a basic REST API endpoint

prompt_templates.title: IDOR Vulnerability Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to test for IDOR vulnerabilities on an API that returns user profile data. The API uses /api/users/{user_id} endpoint. I want to test if I can access other users' profiles.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to specifically test for IDOR vulnerabilities

prompt_templates.title: GraphQL Security Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to audit a GraphQL API for security issues. The endpoint is /graphql. What GraphQL-specific attacks should I test for, and how do I check if introspection is enabled?
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to test a GraphQL API specifically

prompt_templates.title: Full API Penetration Test
prompt_templates.prompt: I have authorization to test an API at https://api.example.com. Create a comprehensive testing plan covering reconnaissance, authentication testing, IDOR, injection, and bypass techniques. Include specific payloads for each test category.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants a comprehensive penetration testing plan

output_examples.input: Help me test for IDOR on /api/v1/invoices/{invoice_id}
output_examples.output:
IDOR Testing Guide:

1. First, access your own invoice to establish baseline
2. Try incrementing the ID: /api/v1/invoices/12345 → /api/v1/invoices/12346
3. Test bypass techniques:
   - Wrap ID in array: {"id":[12345]}
   - JSON wrapper: {"id":{"id":12345}}
   - Parameter pollution: ?invoice_id=12345&invoice_id=12346
4. Test different user contexts if you have multiple accounts
5. Check for horizontal and vertical IDOR (different privilege access)

output_examples.input: What SQL injection payloads work in JSON API requests?

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing any API you do not own
Test on non-production environments when available to avoid disrupting services
Document all findings with proof-of-concept requests and responses for bug reports
Use rate limiting and throttling to avoid overwhelming target APIs during testing
anti_patterns: Do not test production APIs without explicit authorization from the owner
Do not use aggressive scanning techniques that could cause denial of service
Do not assume all endpoints have the same security controls - test each separately
Do not ignore rate limiting - respect the target and back off when limits are hit

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: This skill provides security testing methodology. Using these techniques on APIs you do not own or do not have authorization to test may be illegal. Always get written permission from the target owner before testing.

faq.question: Do I need special tools to use this skill?
faq.answer: This skill works best with a proxy tool like Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, or similar. You will also need understanding of HTTP requests and common web vulnerabilities.

faq.question: Can this skill find all API vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No single skill can find all vulnerabilities. This skill covers common API issues but advanced threats may require specialized tools and techniques. Manual testing combined with automation yields best results.

faq.question: What is the difference between REST and GraphQL testing?
faq.answer: REST and GraphQL have different attack surfaces. GraphQL uses a single endpoint with query language, enabling introspection, batching attacks, and nested query DoS. REST typically has multiple endpoints with different HTTP methods.

faq.question: How do I test APIs that use OAuth or API keys?
faq.answer: Include authentication headers in your requests. Test both authenticated and unauthenticated states. For OAuth, check token validation, refresh token endpoints, and authorization flow for vulnerabilities.

faq.question: What should I do if I find a vulnerability?
faq.answer: Document the finding with clear steps to reproduce, proof of impact, and request/response examples. Submit through the target's bug bounty program responsible disclosure process.
